# **Product Requirements Document** {#product-requirements-document}

Team 14: UC Davis VMTH Cancer Registry  
Authors: [Yugraj Dhillon](mailto:ydhillon@ucdavis.edu), [David Estrella](mailto:dtestrella@ucdavis.edu), [Chun Ho Li](mailto:lchli@ucdavis.edu), [Justin Pak](mailto:jetpak@ucdavis.edu)

## Introduction  {#introduction}

There exists refined and expansive databases for human cancer cases, but the same cannot be said for animals such as dogs and cats. Efforts have been made to create one, but nothing definitive has yet to be created. UC Davis, one of the number one Veterinarian programs in the world has 30+ years of free text clinical data that is unusable currently due to it being unorganized. 

What we aim to accomplish is to organize the clinical data, and to create a cancer registry with a dashboard for researchers to use to identify geographical data to help us understand cancer risks in certain regions. It is not only helpful to know that cancer rates are high or low in a region for dogs and cats, but it can also reveal cancer risks that may lay for humans as well (e.g., we could explore if environmental factors are affecting both humans and animals in an area). This registry will be one of a kind in California, and we hope that other states in this nation will adopt this as well. 

## Competitive Analysis {#competitive-analysis}

We analyzed five similar existing cancer registries:

### Take C.H.A.R.G.E {#take-c.h.a.r.g.e}

| *What they do well* | *Areas for improvement* |
| :---- | :---- |
| Allows the public to upload their dog’s medical records anonymously Informs users of average costs and treatment options for specific cancers in their region Uses the Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 coding system to allow comparison between dogs and humans | Allowing the public to upload records includes a risk of erroneous or incomplete medical records Funded by pharmaceutical company (Jaguar Health) which brings unease as to what is done with the data |

### VMDB {#vmdb}

| *What they do well* | *Areas for improvement* |
| :---- | :---- |
| Receives data from only Veterinary Teaching Hospitals, ensuring accurate diagnoses Operating since 1964 so it has a history of data that can be used to track cancer trends | Uses the SNOMED coding system which requires specific formatting, not scalable to other Veterinarian facilities Requires formal requests and fees to access the database Data is skewed to pets whose owners could afford university specialists |

### SAVSNET {#savsnet}

| *What they do well* | *Areas for improvement* |
| :---- | :---- |
| Receives data in real-time through the electronic health records of participating private vet clinics and labs Uses NLP (PetBERT) and Text Mining to map data to codes Reduces data bias as less specialized clinics can contribute data | Uses the VeNom coding system (UK standard) and SNOMED-CT which are not as scalable Uses data only from the UK |

### Swiss Canine and Feline Cancer Registry {#swiss-canine-and-feline-cancer-registry}

| *What they do well* | *Areas for improvement* |
| :---- | :---- |
| Tracks all cases in a specific geographic population, allowing calculation of true incidence rates Uses the Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 coding system to allow comparison between dogs and humans | Restricted to Switzerland, lack of scalability Maintenance is expensive and labor-intensive due to level of detail |

### ACARCinom {#acarcinom}

| *What they do well* | *Areas for improvement* |
| :---- | :---- |
| Gathers data in a convenient way (through biopsy) which reduces additional work required Uses the Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 coding system to allow comparison between dogs and humans | Requires tumor biopsy for data, leading to undercounting of cancer if a pet is not biopsied |

## Technology Survey {#technology-survey}

### Front-end {#front-end}

Conclusion: We evaluated both React and Angular and we came to the conclusion that React would be the most logical choice. It’s the most supported frontend framework out there. It has strong support for data visualization libraries, it’s easy to integrate with REST APIs, and it’s widely adopted in research environments over angular. 

| React |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Commonly used for websites with interactive elements Relatively easy to pick up | Might be too heavy and can easily end up creating a slow website |

| Angular |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Strong TypeScript support | Steep learning curve Probably overkill for our use case Not the standard in most environments |

### Back-end {#back-end}

Conclusion: NLP pipelines and data processing are primarily Python-based. Coupled with our plan to use BERT, Python is an obvious inclusion for our project (at least on the business logic side). We also plan on using FastAPI for backend development since we felt the FastAPI’s strong points aligned with our team’s goals. Lastly, for the website itself, our plan is to code the website using TypeScript due to its benefits over JavaScript (type safety, tooling support, error catching, etc) making it well suited for a team project.

| FastAPI |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Native support for NLP (BERT, SpaCy, HuggingFace) Easy REST API creation Supports powerful libraries like Pandas and NumPy Widely used in research environments | Slightly slower than node for high-throughput workloads (relevant given the amount of data we’re handling) |

| Node.js/Bun |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Good frontend tooling support Good native ecosystem Since we have no tech debt we don’t have to worry about trying to adopt new tech | New tech so there might be issues Limited ML/NLP support as far as we know Self-contained ecosystem doesn’t matter as much when we still need Python anyways for BERT |

### Database {#database}

Conclusion: We’ll go with PostgreSQL initially since it suits our use case the best, but may switch to a MySQL service depending on how things pan out at the start. For hosting, we selected Supabase as it offers native PostGIS support, built-in authentication and row level security, and is the most cost effective provider available. This takes away the need to self-host while preserving access to all PostgreSQL and PostGIS features. 

| MySQL |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Simple to setup Good performance for read-heavy workloads Native full-text search capabilities | Weaker geospatial support since can’t use PostGIS Less flexible with complex data types |

| PostgreSQL |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Geospatial support via PostGIS extension Native full-text search capabilities Strong data integrity with ACID compliance | Slightly more complex setup than MySQL |

| MongoDB |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Flexible schema, good for inconsistent medical record formats Easy to iterate on data models | Not as good for doing relational queries (e.g., linking patient records, diagnoses, and outcomes to each other) Geospatial features less mature compared to PostGIS extension |

| Elastisearch |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Excellent for doing processes involving full-text search (e.g., pathology reports) Fast aggregations and filtering Good for exploratory data analysis | Complex to maintain |

### API Structuring {#api-structuring}

Conclusion: The clear choice is RESTful APIs to connect the frontend with the backend, it just provides the most simplicity with good performance. It’ll be easy to debug and it’ll provide the analytics endpoints necessary to create the dashboard.

| REST |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Simple Native support in FastAPI Works well with React Minimal learning curve | Can overfetch in some scenarios (mostly a non-issue given our use case). |

| GraphQL |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Flexible queries | More complex, higher learning curves |

| gRPC |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| High performance Real time streaming | Overkill for our use case |

### NLP/Data Extraction {#nlp/data-extraction}

Conclusion: The client wants BERT, so the best option for doing NLP is using BERT. Also using an LLM like ChatGPT for example presents many privacy issues that we do not want to go down. We will be specifically using a fine-tuned PetBERT since it is the only publicly available BERT-family model pretrained specifically on veterinary electronic health records.

| BERT |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| It’s what the client wants us to use. The client is familiar with BERT as well so they’ll have an easier time using the final product when we deliver it to them. | The entire team has to take some time get familiar with BERT (not really a downside so much as a required aspect). |

| LLM APIs (GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini) |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Strong extractions Minimal set-up No hosting required Returns structured JSON output Handles edge cases and ambiguous phrasing with reasoning | Cost Expensive to scale 500 word pathology report is about 650 tokens and would cost \~$16-$30 per batch run, recurring with every uploaded if using GPT-4o with a cost of $0.0025 per 1,024 input tokens Privacy Concerns Inconsist Can produce different labels across runs Latency |

| SpaCy NER |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Lightweight Easy to customize Easy to integrate | Lower accuracy Requires extensive hand-written rules to map to subtypes Handles phrasing variation poorly Not pretrained for veterinary terms Would have to train from scratch Fails to generalize well when encountering unseen report formats |

#### Bert Models {#bert-models}

Conclusion: PetBERT was pretrained on over 400,000 veterinary clinical records from the VetCompass programme. This gives it the needed vocabulary and contextual representations which the other models lack. It outperformed BERT Base and BioBERT on veterinary clinical NLP benchmarks which includes disease classification tasks. Since we need to map raw pathology reports to Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 cancer groups and subtypes, domain-specific pretraining is more important than parameter count or architecture novelty. (More in the Machine Learning/NLP section)

| Model | Parameters | Training Corpus | Veterinary Domain Fit | Notes |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **BERT Base** | 110M | General English (BooksCorpus \+ Wikipedia) | Low | No clinical or veterinary adaptation. Lacks oncology vocabulary. |
| **BioBERT** | 110M | PubMed abstracts \+ PMC full-text | Low–Medium | Strong biomedical NLP, but trained on human research literature. Veterinary terminology is underrepresented. |
| **ClinicalBERT** | 110M | MIMIC-III clinical notes (human ICU) | Low–Medium | Adapted to clinical note style and abbreviations, but human-specific. Animal anatomy and species-specific terms are absent. |
| **BioClinicalBERT** | 110M | PubMed \+ MIMIC-III | Medium | Combines biomedical literature and human clinical notes. Better coverage of medical terminology but still no veterinary adaptation. |
| **PubMedBERT** | 110M | PubMed full-text only (from-scratch pretraining) | Low–Medium | Strong on biomedical abstracts; from-scratch training avoids general-English bias but still lacks veterinary records. |
| **PetBERT** | 110M | Veterinary EHR clinical notes (VetCompass UK) | High | Only model pretrained on veterinary clinical language. Best alignment with VMTH pathology report phrasing, species-specific anatomy, and oncology terminology. |

### 

### Geospatial Analysis {#geospatial-analysis}

Conclusion: We decided that using both for this project ended up being the best choice. GeoPandas for geospatial data processing, and PostGIS for spatial storage and queries. This will allow us to do spatial joins between cancer cases and census tracts. Frontend visualization will be done through react mapping libraries.

| PostGIS (PostgreSQL Extension) |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Stores geometry directly in the database Enables spatial queries Allows aggregations by census tract or region | Lengthy setup |

| GeoPandas |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Python-native Supports spatial joins Integrates easily with Pandas workflow | Slower when working with big datasets |

### Project Management Software {#project-management-software}

Conclusion: We will be using GitHub Projects for now due to its tight integrations, but if it fails to fulfill our needs then we can quickly and easily pivot to a Trello-based solution

| Trello |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Easy to setup More familiar | Some features we want might be paywalled Requires setup for automation (e.g., PM needs to write GitHub Actions) |

| GitHub Projects |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Can be linked with issues on GitHub Comes built in to GitHub Tight integration with GitHub issues | Less familiar (more onboarding/setup) Less features Might not have the features we want |

### CI/CD {#ci/cd}

Conclusion: We will be using GitHub Actions due to its simplicity and integration with GitHub, where our project is stored. It is free to use and is automatically triggered whenever we update our repository.

| Google Cloud Build |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Native integration with GCP services Customizable | Complex Weak ecosystem for non-GCP tasks Requires a billing account |

| GitHub Actions |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Triggered directly by push/PR events Free and simple Contains pre-built actions for GCP and more Secrets management | Slow cold starts Limited debug experience Not optimal for large projects |

### 

### Frontend Hosting {#frontend-hosting}

Conclusion: We will be using Vercel due to its simplicity and integration with Vite/React and GitHub Actions’ CI/CD. It also works well with GitHub Actions which we will be using.

| Firebase Hosting |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Good CDN Fast global delivery Shared ecosystem with GCP for centralized management (billing, console, etc.) | Vite/React Setup requires manual configuration Complex to use Optimized for Firebase’s ecosystem (does not work well with Supabase) |

| Vercel |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Built for Vite/React (repo just needs to be connected) Edge network with excellent global CDN Clean and simple UI Automatic PR previews | Free tier limits Bandwidth Build minute limits Separate system from GCP (another to manage) |

### Backend Hosting {#backend-hosting}

Conclusion: We will be using GCP for the backend and ML worker. It provides the compute flexibility needed for FastAPI and GPU access for PetBERT batch inference, and our model weights are already stored in GCS.

| Google Cloud Platform (GCP) |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Native integration with GCP Batch for ML jobs Flexible compute options Model weights already stored in GCS Has access to educational credits | More complex setup Requires billing account |

| Railway/Render |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Simple container deployment Free/low cost tiers | Limited GPU support Less control over infrastructure  Not suited for ML workloads |

### Authentication {#authentication}

Conclusion: We will be using Supabase’s authentication as it is easy to manage all of the data-related information from one place and because of its simplicity.

| Firebase Authentication |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Allows multiple methods for authentication (SMS, email, etc.) Part of GCP ecosystem (easy to manage) | Does not work well when paired with Supabase for database Requires extra integration work |

| Supabase Authentication |  |
| ----- | ----- |
| **Pros** | **Cons** |
| Works well with Supabase’s database and storage JWTs verified directly by Postgres RLS Self-hostable | Lacks SMS authentication Less pre-built UI components Coupled with Supabase (hard to migrate) |

### 

## System Architecture Overview  {#system-architecture-overview}

![][image1]

We have a three layered approach here that will be able to support cancer data ingestion, natural language processing, geospatial awareness, and interactive visualization. CSV files will form the system’s raw data portion and are stored locally to preserve original records. 

The frontend is implemented using React and TypeScript. This will provide the interface for uploading datasets, exploring cancer case records, and visualizing statistics and geographic distributions. The frontend will communicate with the backend through RESTful API endpoints. For hosting, the frontend is hosted on Vercel. 

The backend is built with FastAPI and SQLAlchemy in Python and serves at the central layer of this application. It organizes its API into seven modules: summary statistics and filters, cancer incidence counts, geospatial data and CalEnviroScreen indicators, time series trends, full text report search, data ingestion and review, and user authentication. Authentication is handled through Supabase JWT’s. Admin access is determined by checking the user’s email against a configured allowlist. 

The NLP worker performs asynchronous extraction using BERT (possible we change models) to identify the cancer type and then those extracted results are written back into PostgreSQL. This will add onto the original records without overwriting any of the raw data.

PostgreSQL will store normalized clinical data and it will allow us to use PostGIS so that we can conduct spatial aggregation. Through that we can do geographical analysis of cancer by county and ZIP code.

We’ll separate the NLP worker from the API layer so that we prevent long running interface tasks from affecting the application responsiveness. This way, we can allow the processing pipeline to scale independently from the user facing services.

Our machine learning implementation lives under the /ml/ directory in the project monorepo and is divided into four main workflows:

1. An annotation pipeline that generates label supervision from diagnosis text.  
2. A production inference pipeline that scores report text against the Vet-ICD taxonomy.  
3. A training pipeline that updates classifiers and adapted backbones.  
4. An evaluation pipeline that measures prediction quality against verified labels.

The NLP service now also includes a local Ollama-backed LLM annotation workflow. The workflow supports label generation and refinement during annotation, but is not the main production classifier used for registry inference. The annotation workflow is used as a fallback option for when the algorithm picks up any keywords (that hints as it being an ICD cancer) but can’t determine what the exact cancer type is. When this happens we run the keywords against the LLM to aid in annotating the diagnosis.

One important goal when designing the architecture was to keep things modular so that ingestion, storage, model execution, and analytics are separated into distinct responsibilities. That separation makes retraining easier, supports future expansion to additional data sources, and provides better privacy control than a production design that depends on external hosted inference services.

In conclusion, the architecture prioritizes the important aspects of a health oriented project, data integrity, privacy, and modularity. This way we can have structured clinical analytics while maintaining an auditable raw data layer. 

### Data Flow {#data-flow}

1\. Raw pathology or report CSV data is uploaded or prepared for ingestion.  
2\. Structured metadata fields and raw free-text report content are preserved so the original record remains auditable.  
3\. Diagnosis text can be passed through the annotation workflow to generate training labels aligned to Vet-ICD-O-canine-1.  
4\. Full pathology report text is processed by the PetBERT-based production pipeline to generate report embeddings and classification scores.  
5\. Predictions are mapped into standardized term, group, and ICD code outputs using the veterinary cancer taxonomy.  
6\. Structured outputs and associated metadata are stored in PostgreSQL.  
7\. Geographic attributes are queried through PostGIS to support regional aggregation and map-based visualization.  
8\. The frontend consumes backend REST endpoints to display case tables, filters, trend summaries, and geographic views.

![][image2]

## Machine Learning/NLP {#machine-learning/nlp}

The project deploys PetBERT as the core production language model. PetBERT is used because it was trained on veterinary electronic health records and is therefore better aligned with veterinary pathology terminology than general-language BERT models. This project needs a model that can better represent animal-specific clinical language, abbreviations, and report phrasing that appear in veterinary oncology records.

The implementation uses PetBERT embeddings as the backbone for classification and structured cancer label prediction. Report text and taxonomy labels are embedded into the same vector space, and those representations are then used by the production pipeline to score likely cancer labels. This makes PetBERT a stronger fit than standard BERT Base, which is trained on general English and lacks veterinary domain adaptation. Human-clinical models such as ClinicalBERT or biomedical literature models such as BioBERT may be useful references, but they are still not trained specifically on veterinary terms.

Externally hosted LLMs are not the primary production path because they create privacy concerns, recurring usage costs, and less deterministic structured-output behavior. The project does include a local LLM-based annotation workflow, but that workflow exists to support annotation and label generation rather than to serve as the main deployed classifier. The current implementation direction is a combination of annotation support, PetBERT-based production inference, and contrastive adaptation plus classifier-based scoring as the strongest internal approach documented to date.  
![][image3]

#### Deployment and Cost Considerations {#deployment-and-cost-considerations}

| Consideration | Detail |
| :---- | :---- |
| **Compute requirement** | PetBERT inference on CPU is feasible for small batches but slow at scale. A GCP Batch job using an N1 instance with 1x NVIDIA T4 GPU (available on GCP as n1-standard-4 \+ T4 accelerator) reduces per-report inference time from \~2–3s (CPU) to \~0.1–0.2s (GPU). |
| **Estimated GCP Batch cost** | A T4 GPU instance on GCP costs approximately $0.35/hr. A batch of 500 pathology reports processed in \~5 minutes costs less than $0.03 per run. For 10,000 reports, estimated cost is under $0.60 per batch job. |
| **Model storage** | Our fine-tuned PetBERT weights are stored in GCS and loaded into the Batch container at job start. No persistent GPU instance is required between jobs. |
| **Fine-tuning** | As labeled VMTH data accumulates, PetBERT can be fine-tuned on VMTH-specific report-label pairs using a classification head. We perform end-to-end fine-tuning of PetBERT as a sequence classifier on the group prediction task and use keyword pipeline outputs as training labels. We extract ground truth labels from a csv containing keyword predictions and pair them with our report text csv file. We treat unmatched cases as uncategorized (Class 0). This is expected to meaningfully improve subtype accuracy beyond zero-shot PetBERT performance. Fine-tuning runs can be triggered as a separate GCP Batch job on an A100 instance if needed. |
| **Cold start latency** | GCP Batch job startup (container pull \+ model load) adds \~60–90 seconds before inference begins. This is acceptable given that report processing is asynchronous and users are notified on completion. |
| **Fallback** | Low-confidence predictions (score below a configurable threshold) are flagged in pathology\_reports for manual review rather than written directly to case\_diagnoses. This prevents low-quality model outputs from corrupting the registry. |

## Product Testing

As we begin to approach the middle and later stages of the project (e.g., bug fixing, polish, etc) and core functionality begins to stabilize, we plan on implementing stress testing and non-functional testing to validate system functionality under conditions that better match real-world conditions. Examples of tests include load testing the backend API and job pipeline to ensure the system can handle multiple concurrent requests, large dataset uploads, or long-running batch jobs without failure. We will evaluate factors such as response time, memory consumption, and database performance under our simulated peak usage scenarios. 

In addition to stress testing, we also plan on addressing other non-functional requirements like security, reliability, and usability through further review and tests. This includes things like verifying role-based access controls, confirming if the system can recover partial failures in the pipeline, and validating that the UI functions across different browsers. 

## Requirements {#requirements}

### User Stories {#user-stories}

**High Priority**

1. As a veterinary researcher, I want to upload pathology report datasets so that the registry can be updated with new cases.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given an authorized user has two valid CSV or XLSX files — Dataset A (clinical notes with \`anon\_id\` and \`Clinical Diagnoses\` columns) and Dataset B (demographics with \`case\_id\`, \`DtOfRq\`, \`Sex\`, \`Species\`, and \`Breed\` columns) — when they upload both files through the dashboard upload UI, then the system accepts the files, creates an ingestion job with \`pending\_review\` status, and displays a job status indicator. When an admin approves the job and processing completes, the new cases appear in the registry and are visible on the map dashboard.  
2. As a veterinary researcher, I want free-text pathology reports to be converted into structured cancer labels so that unstructured records can be analyzed at scale.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a pathology report contains free-text clinical narrative (e.g., "aggressive osteosarcoma of the distal radius"), when PetBERT processes the report, then the system produces a structured output containing at minimum a cancer group and cancer subtype label aligned to the Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 taxonomy, and stores both the raw text and structured output in the database.  
3. As a veterinary researcher, I want each case mapped to Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 terms, groups, and codes so that veterinary cases can be compared consistently.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a pathology report has been processed by the pipeline, when a user views the case record in the dashboard, then the case displays a Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 term, group, and ICD code. Given two reports describing the same cancer type, when both are processed independently, then both are assigned the same Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 code.  
4. As a veterinary researcher, I want to filter records by species, breed, sex, year, and cancer category so that I can study demographic and clinical risk patterns.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given the dashboard is displaying registry data, when a user applies a filter for any combination of sex, breed, or cancer category using the filter controls, then only records matching all selected criteria are shown in the map view and the case count updates to reflect the filtered result. When all filters are cleared, the full dataset is restored. Note: year range and species filters are supported by the backend API but are not yet exposed in the frontend filter UI.  
5. As a veterinary researcher, I want to view diagnoses coded with the Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 coding system to compare with human cancer registry data to determine environmental and other factors involved.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given the registry contains processed cases, when a user views a case record, then the displayed Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 code matches the internationally recognized standard and is sufficient to cross-reference against human cancer registry datasets such as SEER without additional translation.  
6. As a veterinary researcher, I want ambiguous or low-confidence cases to be flagged for review so that uncertain model outputs are not accepted without oversight.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given PetBERT produces a classification score below the configured confidence threshold for a report, when the batch job completes, then the case is stored with a \`low\_confidence\` prediction method and does not appear on the map dashboard. Given an entire upload batch is submitted, when it enters \`pending\_review\` status, then an admin must approve the job through the admin queue before any records from that batch are processed or visible in the registry. Jobs rejected by an admin are set to \`rejected\` status and no records are written.  
7. As a veterinary researcher, I want to upload static data files that will update the registry with the latest patient records without needing a complex API.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given an authorized user has Dataset A (clinical notes CSV/XLSX) and Dataset B (demographics CSV/XLSX) exported from a clinic’s records system, when they upload both files through the dashboard file upload UI, then the system processes the files without requiring the user to call any API directly, write any code, or configure any integration. Once an admin approves the job and processing completes, new records appear in the registry.  
8. As a veterinary researcher, I want to be able to upload free-text notes that will be converted into structured data for analysis.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given Dataset A contains a \`Clinical Diagnoses\` column with raw free-text clinical narrative (e.g., "aggressive osteosarcoma of the distal radius"), when the file is uploaded and the job is approved and processed, then the NLP pipeline extracts structured cancer group and subtype labels from the \`Clinical Diagnoses\` column and stores the structured output alongside the original text. The structured output is queryable and displayable on the dashboard.  
9. As an authorized user, I want to keep all of the uploaded data private and secure to avoid a patient’s data from being traceable, preserving anonymity.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given an unauthenticated user attempts to access the upload interface or any patient record via the frontend or API, then the system returns a 401 Unauthorized response and no patient data is exposed. Given an authenticated user with read-only permissions attempts to write or modify a record directly via the Supabase API, then the RLS policy rejects the request. No owner name, address, or other PII is stored in or returned by any database table or API response.  
10. As a veterinary researcher, I want to view California counties through a heatmap to identify geographically relevant environments.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given the registry contains geocoded case records, when a user opens the map dashboard, then a choropleth heatmap of California counties is displayed where color intensity corresponds to case density. Counties with no cases are visually distinguishable from counties with cases. Hovering over a county displays the county name and case count.  
11. As a veterinary researcher, I want to view a trend line over the years to determine if the prevalence of specific cancers is increasing or decreasing.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given the registry contains cases spanning multiple years, when a user views the trend chart, then a line graph is displayed showing case counts per year. When a user applies a cancer category filter, then the trend line updates to reflect only cases matching that category. The chart renders correctly with as few as two years of data.  
12. As a veterinary researcher, I want to filter visualization data by breed, sex, and tumor type to investigate specific risk factors.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given the map is displaying data, when a user selects a specific breed, sex, or tumor type from the filter controls, then the heatmap updates to reflect only cases matching the selected filters. When multiple filters are combined, only cases satisfying all criteria are shown. Note: the trend chart is not yet implemented — once built, it should also respond to these same filter selections.  
13. As a veterinary researcher, I want the system to flag ambiguous diagnoses for manual review to avoid discarding data.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given a pathology report cannot be confidently classified by PetBERT (score below the configured \`embedding\_min\_sim\` threshold of 0.6), when the batch job completes, then the case is stored with a \`low\_confidence\` prediction method rather than discarded. The record is retained in the database and is not silently dropped. No data is deleted as a result of low model confidence.  
14. As a veterinary researcher, I want the system to handle different types of data input to avoid discarding data.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given an uploaded file contains a mix of fully structured rows, partially structured rows, and rows with only free-text notes, when the pipeline processes the file, then fully structured rows are ingested directly, partially structured rows are ingested with available fields populated, and free-text-only rows are passed through the NLP pipeline. No valid row is silently dropped — rows that cannot be processed are written to a log or flagged for review.  
15. As a veterinary researcher, I want to be able to query with little to no knowledge of the coding system or SQL.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given a user with no knowledge of Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 codes or SQL, when they use the dashboard filter and search controls to find cases by common name (e.g., "bone cancer" or "lymphoma"), then relevant results are returned without requiring the user to enter an ICD code or write a query. The UI exposes human-readable labels, not raw codes.  
16. As a veterinary researcher, I want to be able to view visualized data to efficiently understand key statistics with little effort.  
    1. Acceptance Test: Given the registry contains data, when a user opens the dashboard for the first time without applying any filters, then the map, trend chart, and summary statistics (total cases, top cancer types, most affected breeds) are visible without any additional interaction. All visualizations load within 5 seconds on a standard connection.

**Medium Priority**

1. As a veterinary researcher, I want to export structured registry results so that I can perform downstream statistical analysis outside the application.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a researcher has applied filters to the dashboard, when they click the export button, then a CSV file is downloaded containing all records matching the current filters, including cancer group, subtype, Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 code, species, breed, sex, diagnosis year, and county. The exported file opens correctly in standard tools such as Excel or R without reformatting.  
2. As a veterinary researcher, I want to view temporal trends in cancer prevalence so that I can study how patterns change over time.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given the registry contains cases across multiple years, when a researcher views the trend chart and selects a specific cancer group, then the chart displays year-over-year case counts for that group. When no filter is applied, the chart shows aggregate trends across all cancer types. The chart distinguishes between cancer groups using color or labeling.  
3. As a veterinary researcher, I want to inspect case-level predictions and source report context so that I can assess whether a classification is reasonable.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a researcher selects an individual case from the registry, when the case detail view opens, then it displays the raw pathology report text, the PetBERT-assigned cancer group and subtype, the confidence score, and the Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 code. The researcher can read the source text and compare it against the structured output without leaving the dashboard.  
4. As a veterinary researcher, I want to search and query records without needing to know ICD codes or SQL so that the registry remains accessible to non-technical users.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a researcher enters a plain-language search term (e.g., "mast cell tumor" or "golden retriever lymphoma") into the search bar, when results are returned, then matching cases are displayed using human-readable labels. No ICD code or SQL syntax is required at any point in the search workflow.  
5. As a veterinary researcher, I want the system to preserve raw source text alongside structured outputs so that the registry remains auditable.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a pathology report has been processed and classified, when a researcher queries the \`pathology\_reports\` table or views the case detail panel, then the original unmodified report text is accessible alongside the structured NLP output. The raw text is never overwritten or deleted by the pipeline.  
6. As a veterinary researcher, I want the system to support non-cancer or uncategorized cases so that the registry does not force every record into an incorrect cancer label.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a pathology report describes a non-cancerous finding (e.g., benign cyst, inflammatory lesion), when the pipeline processes the report, then the case is stored with a label indicating non-cancer or uncategorized rather than being assigned an incorrect cancer subtype. These cases are excluded from cancer-specific visualizations by default but remain accessible in the registry.  
7. As a veterinary researcher, I want to compare patterns across tumor groups so that I can investigate how different cancer categories behave across regions and populations.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given the dashboard is displaying data, when a researcher selects two or more tumor groups for comparison, then the map and trend chart display data for each selected group using distinct visual encoding (color, line style). The researcher can identify geographic or temporal differences between groups from the visualization without exporting data.

**Low Priority**

1. As a project stakeholder, I want the architecture to support onboarding additional clinics in the future so that the registry can expand beyond one institution.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a new clinic provides a dataset in a different format than the existing VMTH data, when the dataset is ingested through the upload pipeline, then the system processes the file without requiring changes to the core application code. Field mapping or format normalization is handled at the ingestion layer. New clinic records are tagged with a source identifier and appear in the registry alongside existing records.  
2. As a project stakeholder, I want model quality to improve as more labeled data becomes available so that the registry becomes more reliable over time.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a set of manually reviewed and approved case labels has accumulated in the system, when a fine-tuning job is triggered on the accumulated labels, then the updated model produces classification scores on a held-out validation set that are equal to or better than the previous model version. The previous model version is retained and can be restored if accuracy regresses.  
3. As a researcher, I want the registry to support future downstream research workflows so that its structured outputs remain useful beyond the dashboard itself.  
   1. Acceptance Test: Given a researcher wants to use registry data in an external tool, when they access the export or API endpoint, then the structured output includes Vet-ICD-O-canine-1 codes, cancer group, subtype, species, breed, sex, diagnosis year, and county in a machine-readable format (CSV or JSON). The schema is documented sufficiently for a researcher unfamiliar with the system to interpret and use the data.

## Prototyping Code {#prototyping-code}

Github URL: [https://github.com/ECS-193A-Team-14/UC-Davis-VMTH-Cancer-Registry](https://github.com/ECS-193A-Team-14/UC-Davis-VMTH-Cancer-Registry) 

## Technologies Employed {#technologies-employed}

**Programming Languages:** Python (NLP), TypeScript (Frontend)  
**Backend:** FastAPI  
**Frontend:** React  
**Database:** PostgreSQL (Implemented via Supabase)  
**API Structuring:** REST  
**NLP/Data Extraction:** (Pet)BERT (+ LLM Solution)  
**Geospatial Analysis:** PostGIS/GeoPandas  
**Hosting:** Vercel Pro (frontend), Supabase (database), GCP (backend \+ ML worker)  
**Project Management Software:** Trello (used over GitHub Project for faster iteration and turnover)

## Real-World Constraint Analysis {#real-world-constraint-analysis}

### Cost {#cost}

The majority of project costs will be incurred during the development phase, primarily due to the computational resources required to train the machine learning model on client-provided data. During deployment, costs will mainly consist of maintaining the server infrastructure, which is expected to be relatively low. The frontend is hosted on Vercel’s paid plan as it was the most cost efficient option. Unfortunately due to the github repository being private for privacy concerns, we must move on from the free tier to paid tier for production. The database is hosted on Supabase’s free tier currently with the possibility of moving to the next tier if volume exceeds the limit. This will avoid the upfront costs of database hosting. As the platform expands to include additional clinics or datasets, periodic retraining of the model will be necessary, resulting in additional computational expenses. The backend and ML worker are hosted on GCP, with cost scaling based on compute usage during ingestion and classification workloads. 

### Space {#space}

All infrastructure is cloud hosted, Vercel for the frontend, GCP for the backend and ML worker, and Supabase for the database, making on-site hardware unnecessary. 

### Security {#security}

Any portion of the platform that allows clients to upload sensitive patient data will be protected through authentication mechanisms such as password-restricted access. Supabase’s built in authentication and row level security policies enforce access control at the database level, making sure that direct queries abide by authorization rules. There is role based access control via Supabase that restricts the review queue to authorized users. Uploaded data will not be publicly accessible, and access will be limited strictly to authorized users.

### Privacy {#privacy}

All data used for training the model, as well as any future datasets, will be fully de-identified prior to use. As a result, no personally identifiable information (PII) will be stored or processed, minimizing potential privacy risks and ensuring compliance with data protection expectations.

### Scalability {#scalability}

While we have access to extensive records from a single clinic for training, we anticipate a potential decrease in model accuracy when applying the system to clinics from different locations. This is likely due to variations in diagnostic writing styles among veterinarians. To mitigate this issue, the model would need to be retrained or fine-tuned using data from new clinics, allowing it to adapt as the registry expands. On the infrastructure side, Supabase supports vertical scaling, allowing the database to grow with the registry without requiring the team to migrate to another provider.

### Maintainability {#maintainability}

To ensure long-term maintainability, the development team will adhere to modern software engineering principles such as **SOLID** and **KISS**. Comprehensive documentation will be provided to allow future developers or clients to update, debug, and extend the platform as needed.

## Social/Legal Aspect of the Product  {#social/legal-aspect-of-the-product}

The cancer registry for cats and dogs provides meaningful social value by offering researchers in the veterinary field access to an interactive dashboard with a geospatial interface. This platform would represent the first comprehensive cancer registry for cats and dogs in California. By centralizing and visualizing veterinary cancer data, the registry would support researchers in identifying spatial and temporal patterns in cancer cases among companion animals. Additionally, the registry may enable exploratory analysis of potential correlations between cancer trends in cats and dogs and those observed in human populations, contributing to broader public and comparative health research.

From a legal perspective, the client will retain full ownership and rights to all data used for model training and analysis. All records included in the registry will be de-identified prior to use, and no personally identifiable patient information will be made publicly accessible under any circumstances. This ensures compliance with data privacy expectations while allowing the data to be used responsibly for research purposes.

## Glossary of Terms {#glossary-of-terms}

- **API (Application Programming Interface) \-** A set of rules that allows different software components to communicate with each other.  
- **Angular \-** A development platform, built on TypeScript. As a platform, Angular includes: A component-based framework for building scalable web applications. A collection of well-integrated libraries that cover a wide variety of features, including routing, forms management, client-server communication, and more.  
- **Asynchronous Processing \-** A technique where long running tasks are executed separately so the main application remains responsive.  
- **BERT (Bidirectional Encoder Representations from Transformers) \-** A machine learning model used for understanding and extracting information from natural language text.  
- **Bun \-** An all-in-one toolkit for JavaScript and TypeScript apps.  
- **Elastisearch \-** A distributed search and analytics engine, scalable data store and vector database optimized for speed and relevance on production-scale workloads.  
- **FastAPI \-** A python-based web framework used to create backend APIs, well suited for data-driven applications.  
- **GeoPandas \-** An open-source Python library that extends pandas data structures for working with geospatial vector data.  
- **GitHub Projects \-** An adaptable collection of items that you can view as a table, a kanban board, or a roadmap and that stays up-to-date with GitHub data.  
- **GraphQL \-** An open-source query language and server-side runtime that specifies how clients should interact with application programming interfaces (APIs).  
- **MongoDB \-** An open source, nonrelational database management system (DBMS) that uses flexible documents instead of tables and rows to process and store various forms of data.  
- **NLP (Natural Language Processing) \-** A field of artificial intelligence focused on extracting meaning from human language text.  
- **NLP Worker \-** A separate processing component responsible for running NLP models asynchronously without blocking user requests.  
- **Node.js \-** An open-source, cross-platform JavaScript runtime environment.  
- **PostGIS \-** A PostgreSQL extension that adds support for geographic objects and spatial queries.  
- **PostgreSQL \-** An open-source relational database used to store structured application data.  
- **React \-** A JavaScript framework used to build interactive web user interfaces.  
- **REST (Representational State Transfer) \-** A common architectural style for web APIs that uses standard HTTP methods to exchange data between a client and server.  
- **Trello \-** A web-based, kanban-style, list-making application for team project management.
